learning_outcomes.description: Analyse complex organisational and societal challenges using wellbeing polarity leadership and systems-thinking frameworks.
Develop strategies for sustainable and adaptive change across diverse stakeholder contexts.
Reflect on your leadership assumptions, behaviours, and patterns to support ongoing personal and professional development.
Justify leadership insights and recommendations to diverse audiences using evidence-based argument.
Collaboratively evaluate leadership resistance and competing perspectives in complex change contexts.

assessments.task_description:
For full details regarding this Assessment, including what is required, formatting and marking rubric, please see the EIBS7304 Study & Assessment Guide located on the Course Blackboard site.

Purpose:

To help students gain deep theoretical knowledge of a systems approach to wellbeing in regional renewal.

 Task:

Students will be given a scenario and tasked to write an essay response putting forward a proposal to remedy the scenario. In their answer, students must use prescribed concepts from weeks 1-6 of the course.

Time: 10 minutes planning; 1 hour 20 minutes essay writing time

Conditions:

  • Individual-only assessment;
  • Closed book, in-class, exam conditions;
  • Maximum 5 pages, 12 pt font, 1.5 line spacing; references not required.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
For full details regarding this Assessment, including what is required, formatting and marking rubric, please see the EIBS7304 Study & Assessment Guide located on the Course Blackboard site.

Purpose:

To learn how to convert ‘leadership issues’ into the better leveraging of wellbeing polarities.

Task:

Working as a group of 4 or 5, conduct a 15 minute video presentation covering the following material that tests your ability to apply a wellbeing polarity approach:

  1. Identify and describe a Leader you can interview or have chosen to study. Why this Leader?;
  2. Identify a significant leadership issue faced by this Leader by either interviewing them or through resource material (the issue can be business, political, local, global);
  3. Describe the Issue based on answers to:
  • What is a major issue that is occupying your attention?
  • What negative symptoms do you experience as a result of this issue?
  • What is the current way of trying to address the issue?
  • What makes it complicated to address?
  • What are you trying to achieve by addressing the issue?

Answers to these questions can come from direct interview or inferred from resource material (state your sources).

  1. Convert the information from the above five bullet points into a wellbeing polarity map for a particular stakeholder including the two wellbeing poles, upsides and downsides, actions steps and early warning systems. Present the map justifying your entries along the way;
  2. Discuss one or two main defences which might arise from leaders at conventional stages of consciousness when considering if they should adopt your wellbeing polarity approach and how might you overcome these defences?
  3. Offer your Reflections on the wellbeing polarity approach. Does it have value? How? Why or why not? What was challenging?
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit the assessment via the link on the course Learn.UQ site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
For full details regarding this Assessment, including what is required, formatting and marking rubric, please see the EIBS7304 Study & Assessment Guide located on the Course Blackboard site.

1 page stating “Who Am I” - Follow structure in EIBS7304 Study and Assessment Guide

2 page summary of required reading and leadership style test result graphic

Summarise the main points from chapters 3 and 8 of ‘Mastering Leadership’ (2 pages). Include your test graphic result as a third separate page.

1 page Identification and justification of Reactive leadership issue of focus* - your Reactive part Story

Select a personal reactive part based on your test results in the categories of complying, protecting or controlling or another key issue for you. Write your ‘Reactive part Story’ including:

  • When the reactive part strikes what does it say in your head? What thoughts enter your head?
  • What does the reactive part say about you in the moment? (what thoughts does it make you think about yourself?)
  • How strong is its force on you?
  • Why is it hard to resist?
  • What does the reactive part make you want to do? (behaviour?)
  • What events in your past may have built this reactive part?
  • What beliefs did you form at the time of these events?

1 Page Rate Your Reactive part

  • Numerical scoring of frequency, degree of control, degree of disruption, desire to change

1 page structure of the reactive part underpinning reactive leadership issue

  • Do a cognitive conceptualization of situation-emotion-automatic thoughts and beliefs-behaviour based on your Reactive part Story

1 page revised story about your ‘opposite’

  • Write one page including:
  • What is the opposite behaviour to that dictated by your reactive part?
  • In not doing the opposite behaviour what are you trying to avoid? Why?
  • What beliefs do you have about the opposite and why its no good?
  • What assumptions underpin those beliefs?
  • What beliefs and assumptions about your opposite are worth challenging and changing?
  • How can you let yourself experience your opposite in the course?

Changing your relationship with your reactive part and opposite

  • Using the diary outline provided in the Study & Assessment Guide record how you have worked on your reactive part and practiced your leadership opposite

1 page Revised Who Am I

  • After undergoing all of the above how does it change how you see yourself and what does this mean for how you will ‘show up’ in the world.
  • Write one page including:
  • Reflect on the whole personal development side of the course and all the things you’ve done and read;
  • What theories, readings, tasks impacted you the most;
  • Discuss how you have changed;
  • Discuss how you will ‘show up’ differently in the world as a result.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit the assessment via the link on the course Learn.UQ site.

learning_activities.topic:
Intro to Course

In this first class, students are introduced to the purpose of the course: to build entrepreneurial leaders fit for 21st century purpose. Modern leaders must be able to process considerable complexity if they are to act responsibly, sustainably and resiliently. The class takes students through the regional focus of the course, explaining the current demand for regional leaders who are entrepreneurial. Students will also be introduced to the leader development side of the course where they will undergo their own personal journey of self-awareness and expansion.

Students will also be introduced to the leader development side of the course where they will undergo their own personal journey of self-awareness and expansion.

learning_activities.topic:
Regional Renewal: The challenges of the modern leader

The focus of this class is to show students the complexity of real-life modern regional leadership. Students will learn how things have shifted from just economic development to placemaking. They will also learn the new skills required by regional leaders engaged in placemaking
learning_activities.topic_title: Regional Renewal: The challenges of the modern leader
learning_activities.topic_description: Regional Renewal: The challenges of the modern leader The focus of this class is to show students the complexity of real-life modern regional leadership. Students will learn how things have shifted from just economic development to placemaking. They will also learn the new skills required by regional leaders engaged in placemaking

learning_activities.topic:
Regional Renewal & Community Gridlock

This class shows students 1) a successful case of regional renewal and what it can look like and 2) a case where renewal has stalled resulting in community gridlock
learning_activities.topic_description: Regional Renewal & Community Gridlock This class shows students 1) a successful case of regional renewal and what it can look like and 2) a case where renewal has stalled resulting in community gridlock

learning_activities.topic:
Eight Component Model of Regional Wellbeing

After the previous context-setting weeks, students are now introduced to the first core component of the wellbeing polarity leadership (WPL) framework. WPL is advanced as a way to help develop leaders who can navigate the placemaking, gridlock and polarisation challenges of the 21st century.

This week students will also begin their leader development journey. They will be introduced to the concept and research of leader versatility
learning_activities.topic_description: Eight Component Model of Regional Wellbeing After the previous context-setting weeks, students are now introduced to the first core component of the wellbeing polarity leadership (WPL) framework. WPL is advanced as a way to help develop leaders who can navigate the placemaking, gridlock and polarisation challenges of the 21st century. This week students will also begin their leader development journey. They will be introduced to the concept and research of leader versatility

learning_activities.topic:
Leader Mental Models 1 - understanding different leader worldviews

Efforts at regional renewal can stall with an inability to cohere stakeholders. This takes understanding of how different leaders make sense of the world. This week students are introduced to the stages of consciousness model from adult developmental psychology as a way to understand how conflicting opinions happen and how to work with them.

Students also are introduced to the leadership circle model as a next step in their personal journey. This model explains and shows how we get stuck as individuals and where we need to build greater adaptive capacity.
learning_activities.topic_title: Leader Mental Models 1 - understanding different leader worldviews
learning_activities.topic_description: Leader Mental Models 1 - understanding different leader worldviews Efforts at regional renewal can stall with an inability to cohere stakeholders. This takes understanding of how different leaders make sense of the world. This week students are introduced to the stages of consciousness model from adult developmental psychology as a way to understand how conflicting opinions happen and how to work with them. Students also are introduced to the leadership circle model as a next step in their personal journey. This model explains and shows how we get stuck as individuals and where we need to build greater adaptive capacity.

learning_activities.topic:
Leader Mental Models 2 - Systems thinking

In the first half of this week, we build on the theory of leader stages of consciousness introduced last week. Students are shown how most leaders are at 'conventional' stages while 21st century complexity requires a 'post-conventional' mindset. This week specifies the differences between the two.

In the second half of this week, students begin to unpack their own Leadership Circle diagnostic assessment. This helps students to identify the leadership issue they will work on in the course as part of Assessment 3.
learning_activities.topic_description: Leader Mental Models 2 - Systems thinking In the first half of this week, we build on the theory of leader stages of consciousness introduced last week. Students are shown how most leaders are at 'conventional' stages while 21st century complexity requires a 'post-conventional' mindset. This week specifies the differences between the two. In the second half of this week, students begin to unpack their own Leadership Circle diagnostic assessment. This helps students to identify the leadership issue they will work on in the course as part of Assessment 3.

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment 1 Preparation

The class this week is dedicated to giving students the time and space to prepare for Assessment 1. Students will be given exercises in class to help prepare them.
learning_activities.topic_description: Assessment 1 Preparation The class this week is dedicated to giving students the time and space to prepare for Assessment 1. Students will be given exercises in class to help prepare them.
Assessment 1 Students will undertake an in-class Assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to Polarities

Students now move to learning about a core feature of the wellbeing polarity leadership model: polarities. This class is dedicated to explaining the topic, heading towards the knowledge and skills needed for Assessment 2 in the course.

Students will learn to see polarities in regional development but also within themselves as leaders. Students will learn how their leadership circle result exposes a key polarity limiting their current leadership potential.
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to Polarities Students now move to learning about a core feature of the wellbeing polarity leadership model: polarities. This class is dedicated to explaining the topic, heading towards the knowledge and skills needed for Assessment 2 in the course. Students will learn to see polarities in regional development but also within themselves as leaders. Students will learn how their leadership circle result exposes a key polarity limiting their current leadership potential.

learning_activities.topic:
Polarity Mapping

This week students will learn a practical tool for applying the concept of polarities to real-world problems
learning_activities.topic_description: Polarity Mapping This week students will learn a practical tool for applying the concept of polarities to real-world problems

learning_activities.topic:
Creating wellbeing value through polarities

This week now connects the 3 essential components of the wellbeing polarity leadership model: wellbeing, stages of consciousness and polarities. Students will learn a practical tool for transforming current regional/organizational issues into wellbeing value.
learning_activities.topic_description: Creating wellbeing value through polarities This week now connects the 3 essential components of the wellbeing polarity leadership model: wellbeing, stages of consciousness and polarities. Students will learn a practical tool for transforming current regional/organizational issues into wellbeing value.

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment 2 & 3 Preparation

Time is dedicated in this class to working on Assessments 2 and 3.

Assessment 2 & 3 Workshop

Working through the steps of Assessments 2 & 3 with an example
